item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Noting that images are indeed compliant with the site's license, and the article tags have been updated to reflect this.

item.title: Re: Update
item.content:encoded: Should the image caption for the ant have the SCP number? Currently it has XXXX.

item.title: Update
item.content:encoded:
I've updated this article to modernize the formatting and put in some new images, which are attributed in my (edited) original post. I've changed the collapsibles for a tabview module, adding a new image to the first tab to pad that tab out a little and provide a visual aid.

I never really liked the collapsible solution, which I hit upon because presenting all the information about the two effects back-to-back on the page looked clunky and bloated to my eyes. A tabview seems like a great solution retrospectively, since it divvies up that information well without having a collapsible that opens to reveal a bunch of plain text. Do let me know if you think the new format detracts, though, because I can easily change it back. The pictures are going to stay though - the page was always supposed to open with a picture, after all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty good suggestion now I come back to it. Implemented.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems like this one has the potential for serious abuse. I can imagine a researcher trying to steal and use. Suppose a loved one of yours was murdered, the possibility that killing the one responsible might undo the murder would have a very strong appeal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the last addendum is really nice, in my opinion. It may remove some mystery surrounding it, but it trades in utter weirdness that helps make the article have more staying power.

The society which made use of SCP-1509 appears to have had a strong association between ants, cockatoos, and the concept of death and rebirth, possibly as a result of SCP-1509.

Like, this is just fucking bonkers, and it's so cool as a result. The final bit after "rebirth" is probably not needed because that can be insinuated, but as a whole I just really enjoy this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a really interesting idea! There's a few things that could be done to to improve it (grammatical errors, some of the details are kind of confusing and muddled and in some areas it feels like its a bit too much, but that may just be me) but overall this is really interesting!! Great job!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One minor issue to point out: "old age" is not a scientifically recognized cause of death; there's always a more direct and specific cause to be noted (such as organ failure, etc.). Perhaps saying "natural causes" would be better, since that's a catch-all umbrella term, and fits the contrast between the "victim resurrecting" causes of death.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That final paragraph really takes me out of the mood. It's waaaay too in your face; interesting ideas but would have been better if spaced out rather than as a straight infodump. No-voting.

item.title: Image Removal
item.content:encoded: SCP-1509's image was removed because we could not properly determine whether or not the image was properly licensed or attributed. It may be added once again if/when proper attribution or proper use under CC guidelines is confirmed. Please contact Live2Die or another member of the Image Team if you have questions.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I would love to know what would happen if the blade was used on a larger mammal, such as an elephant. The potential for larger wounds has me curious.

That being said, also the effects of decaptitation/ limb severance.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Absolutely fantastic. I was gonna make a knife that made you bleed spiders but this is much better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me that a particularly desperate researcher might try to exchange their life for that of a dead loved one with this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A bit too much going on for my tastes but some interesting ideas; upvoted. Spotted one typo:

"SCP-1509 is kept in a secure storage locker a Storage Site-08."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Awesome except for one problem
If the deceased person cannot remember anything prior to their death how can they claim to remember or know said person?
other than that, upvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
To me, it seemed like "crazy shit mixed up", but the addendum cleared it up (I mean the ants and people crawling out of wounds). Also - releasing to general population? I dunno, that doesn't seem like Foundation etiquette at all (even though I know they have done this already in a few SCPs.)
Not quite for an upvote, but I liked it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Creepy and the parts of it come together well. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The initial effect, the infinite wound, was pretty good all by itself, but the added effects make this even better. Weird and creepy, and a good article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great, and would be one of my favorites if not for the addendum. I had my own assumptions going, curiosity, and then the freedom to fill in the blanks was completely taken away.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too late.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is weird and makes no sense, and I love it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Creepy idea, especially the concept of "birthing" one's former victims.

+ Nitpicks
- Nitpicks

All objects discharged from the wound are to be contained by medical staff. All SCP-1509-1 instances are to be contained by medical staff, and destroyed.

Redundant, because SCP-1509-1 instances are objects discharged from the wound.

until the effects of SCP-1509 ceased

Use either "have ceased" or "cease"

63 centimetres in length, with a steel blade 48cm in length and 11 millimetres

less than 7cm in width

Inconsistent measurement abbreviation

it has an unidentifiably large

This sounds weird to me, I would have used "indeterminately large" instead. Your call.

This phenomenon appears to only occur from outside inspection of the wound itself- the subject is not affected adversely by this anomaly, and the anomaly will not appear in medical imaging. Any foreign objects placed inside the wound and abandoned will disappear if they are placed any further into the wound than the ‘wound’ should physically be able to penetrate.

This paragraph has wording which is a little awkward. My suggestion:

This phenomenon is only apparent in direct inspection of the wound- the anomaly appears to be a typical cut to medical imaging, and subject does not appear to be adversely affected beyond being cut. Any object placed into the wound more deeply than should be possible will disappear if abandoned.

will release a group Pharaoh Ants (Monomorium phaeronis) ,

Should be "a group of Pharoah Ants" with no space after the closing parenthesis.

for the subject, which

Since we've established the subject must be human, I think "who" is appropriate instead of "which"

Instances of SCP-1509-2 show anomalous … should not be able to accommodate its size.

"Instances" is plural, and "its" is singular. I'd recommend rewording this sentence so that the instance is singular, "Each instance of SCP-1509-2 shows anomalous… ", but changing "its size" to "their sizes" could also work.

is universally fatal to the subject … they will quickly

on a subject who has committed murder … typically one of their victims

"The subject" is singular, "they" is plural. I'd recommend changing "their" to "the subject," but that may be too repetitive. You might want to reword these in some other way.

by an unknown society

We sure know a lot about an unknown society! Maybe "previously unknown" would work better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The wounds don't just go to nowhere. Below the surface, there's an infinite tunnel of torn flesh. Blood can get out of the wound because, on the surface, it's like any other wound- the walls of the wound will bleed, not just the 'bottom' of it. Of course, if you orientated your body so the wound faced up, the blood would pour down the spacial tear.

As for the small grammar stuff, and all similar problems that people have pointed out: I'll do it tomorrow. I don't want to clog up recent edits.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Initially, the wound heals extremely slowly, although blood flow from the wound is significantly lower than would be expected.

Like Effect 1509-1, the wound will typically heal at very slow rate, but blood loss from the wound will be significantly lower than what would normally be inspected of such a wound.

The "although" and "but" makes it sound like that the lower blood flow should make the wound heal faster.

And unrelated to grammar: how is any blood flowing out of the wound, since the inside of the wound is empty space?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed a missing space for you. I quite like this. Upvote for you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A double handful of typos and a few grammer gaffs, but nothing painful to read.

Ants crawling out of the wound is just screwed up, well done.

Is there a real culture you are basing this on? I'm a little curious about the mytholigical relationship of cockatoos, ants, and death.

It doesn't feel to me like there are too many effects here. There are quite a few, but to my mind they all fit together in some not-quite-seeable way. Like, the wounds are infinite because they are actually doorways to some realm of the dead. Having said that, if the consensus is that the effects need thinning and you decide to agree; the extra-dimensionality is the least neccessary of the batch.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
to speed to process

Pretty sure this is a typo.

Other than that, I liked it and upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Removed the stuff relating to the funerary figures. The only effects now are infinite wounds, ants and dead people. Hopefully that's improved the article somewhat.

Also, the laptop I use to write this sucks, and kept reformatting my writing and merging words. Looks like I didn't get all of them when I read through the preview. Those problems should be fixed now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm with Dvwxan. There's a lot of stuff going on here when what it seems the main horror is open wounds, ants crawling on out of them, and people bursting from the injuries. It seems to be with a lot of effects for no real reason. Like the concept, not the execution here. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I took that as a dramatic pause ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nice promising idea, although perhaps too many aspects thrown in? Infinite wound depth, ants, accelerated healing, figurines, emergence of dead people, and in the last line we learn the thing doesn't age! I suggest losing the comment about aging, and the figurines.

Also, 1509-2 are "genetically similar to normal humans" but also "genetically identical to … a deceased person". This implies the deceased person was not, strictly, genetically a normal human.

There is a rogue line-break between "unconsciousness, followed by" and "death."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Decent SCP - but a few technical errors - were you using NotePad? Because some spaces are missing between words.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Weird and creepy, plus ants from wounds always ick me out!

The only thing I'm not sure about are the bodies being replaced by funerary figurines; it just seems a little… odd.

Upvote from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Right, my latest SCP is up! I tried for a little more 'horror' with this one than my previous attempts. Not sure if I pulled it off.

EDIT: I have overhauled the formatting of the two effects, since I now think a tabview module is a more appealing way to structure the information than a pair of collapsibles.

I have also replaced the earlier, now-removed non-compliant image, and added an additional image in the description.

  • 'Knife (Parang) with Sheath MET 36.25.840a b 001 Apr2017.jpg' originates from this page from the website of the Metropolitan Museum of Art, was originally created by Edward Hunter, and has been dedicated to the public domain.
  • 'Monomorium pharaonis casent0173986 profile 1.jpg' originates from this www,antweb.org page. It was originally taken by April Nobile, and is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International licence.

I hope this helps make the article a little more visually appealing. This is also the first time I've tried to properly cite image attributions so if I've messed up let me know.
